findings.finding: In debitEscrow, the second readEscrow (line 775) re-reads the same file that was just written. In a single-process scenario without concurrent writes, this second read will always return the same value that was just written. The read-after-write pattern only catches races from other processes writing to the same file between the write and re-read.
findings.reason: This is the design from Decision 7 (tech-spec). The double-read pattern is intentional to catch rare concurrent process scenarios. It is an accepted limitation for MVP.

findings.finding: Attaching debitEscrow directly on the router object is unconventional. While it works in practice (and the BountyRouter type makes it safe), a more standard approach would be to export it as a separate function or return it alongside the router.
findings.reason: The approach follows the existing pattern of the project (dependency injection via closure). Changing to a separate export would require modifying webchat.ts mounting code. The BountyRouter type provides type safety. Acceptable for MVP.

findings.finding: The unit tests duplicate the escrow logic rather than importing it from bounty-api.ts. This means changes to bounty-api.ts logic will not automatically fail unit tests.
findings.reason: This is by design per the task spec: 'автономные, без HTTP, файловая система через моки'. Since bounty-api.ts is TypeScript and tests are plain JS, importing directly would require compilation. The duplicated logic tests the same contract. Integration tests (test_bounty_campaigns.js) test the actual compiled code.

summary: Implementation follows the task spec precisely. Escrow interfaces, endpoints, and debit function with pre-check + rollback all match the tech-spec Data Models and Decision 7. Code style is consistent with existing bounty-api.ts patterns. No blocking issues.
